retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Monitoring, Surveillance, and Reporting
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring, surveillance, and reporting are essential to improve access to quality services, respond to outbreaks, and determine progress toward HIV prevention goals across various populations. In collaboration with local or state public health authorities, clinicians can contribute to these efforts by fully completing reporting forms where required and participating in efforts to streamline and improve surveillance. In 2014, UNAIDS proposed a set of global "90-90-90" HIV prevention targets for 2020. 90% of all people living with HIV will know their HIV status. 90% of all people with diagnosed HIV infection will receive sustained ART. 90% of all people receiving ART will have viral suppression.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Monitoring, Surveillance, and Reporting
retrieved_docs.content: The WHO and country and state disease control and prevention centers, such as the US CDC, are primary data sources for monitoring HIV trends and progress toward goals. Behavioral and clinical characteristics of adults diagnosed with HIV infection are followed in the United States by the Medical Monitoring Project. Instituted in 2005, it is a cross-sectional, nationally representative, complex sample survey.[CDC. HIV Surveillance Report 2021 Cycle. 2023] The CDC's AtlasPlus allows practitioners to explore national HIV, hepatitis, STI, and tuberculosis data relevant to their population.[CDC. NCCHHSTP AtlasPlus. 2023] All data from 2020 and 2021 must be interpreted in the context of decreased case surveillance and access to HIV services during the COVID-19 pandemic.[CDC. HIV Basic Statistics. 2023]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Monitoring, Surveillance, and Continuous Quality Improvement
retrieved_docs.content: The projected impact is fewer than 370,000 people acquiring HIV and fewer than 250,000 people dying from HIV worldwide in 2025. The WHO and country and state disease control and prevention centers, such as the United States CDC, are primary data sources for monitoring HIV trends and progress toward goals. Behavioral and clinical characteristics of adults with HIV are followed in the United States by the Medical Monitoring Project. Instituted in 2005, this is a cross-sectional, nationally representative, complex sample survey. The CDC's AtlasPlus allows clinicians to explore national HIV, hepatitis, sexually transmitted infections, and tuberculosis data relevant to their population.[CDC. NCCHHSTP AtlasPlus 2023. ] All data from 2020 and 2021 must be interpreted in the context of decreased case surveillance and services during the COVID-19 pandemic. Consequently, clinicians can improve care and contribute to population health improvements by completing required documentation, participating in initiatives to streamline surveillance, and engaging in continuous program quality improvement within their practice setting utilizing public health data.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Monitoring, Surveillance, and Reporting
retrieved_docs.content: The National HIV/AIDS Strategy [US Gov. National HIV/AIDS Strategy. 2023] and the Ending the HIV Epidemic in the U.S. initiative aims to reduce new HIV transmissions in the United States by 90% by 2030, prioritizing the reduction of HIV-related health disparities and health inequities and improving well-being for people who live with HIV.[CDC. Ending the HIV epidemic. 2023]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Monitoring, Surveillance, and Reporting
retrieved_docs.content: Adoption by the United Nations General Assembly committed member countries to these targets via a Political Declaration on HIV and AIDS. In 2021, UN member states adopted ambitious yet achievable 95-95-95 targets for 2025 based on data demonstrating good progress across countries, advances in therapeutic options and implementation science, and new insights into inequities. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The HIV care continuum illustrates opportunities for intervention at each step and serves as a tool to motivate healthcare providers to improve patient outcomes. HIV management can occur in various settings, including primary care practices, infectious disease specialty clinics, federally qualified health centers, and dedicated HIV care facilities. Any practice offering HIV screening should establish a formal relationship with a treatment provider and implement a process for confirming access to care following diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Reporting
retrieved_docs.content: Reporting ensures results are promptly and confidentially reported to the Florida Department of Health to allow for contact follow-up and other public health activities, such as surveillance. A positive test result or other diagnosis of HIV or AIDS must be reported within 2 weeks using the system developed by the CDC or an equivalent system to ensure confidentiality. The Department of Health may fine anyone who fails to report HIV or AIDS up to $500 for each offense, and a regulatory agency is informed of the violation. Similar laws exist on these ethical principles in other settings worldwide, which are important to reference when treating 1 or more patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Monitoring, Surveillance, and Continuous Quality Improvement
retrieved_docs.content: Collecting and using reliable, granular, and timely data are essential for improving team performance and population health. Using data means identifying at-risk clients, setting targets, and developing strategies. UNAIDS first proposed a set of global 90-90-90 HIV prevention targets for HIV in 2014 by countries of the United Nations General Assembly. Although these goals were not reached, many countries made significant progress. Setting 95-95-95 goals for 2025, the goals were updated in 2020 and adopted by the United Nations General Assembly in 2021 and include: 95% of all people with HIV who know their status 95% of all people who have HIV and receive antiretroviral therapy 95% of all patients on ART who achieve viral suppression

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The HIV care continuum highlights the ongoing need to expand routine screening and ensure timely linkage to care to improve national HIV outcomes. [14] Strengthening each step in the continuum not only improves individual patient outcomes but also contributes to broader public health goals.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians must be aware of any rules and regulations regarding HIV screening, testing, reporting, and managing HIV in the jurisdiction where they practice. These rules and regulations vary widely. In some jurisdictions, rules and regulations may exist as public health acts and follow a progressive enforcement framework; in others, they may be incorporated into criminal law and carry significant penalties.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: To identify undiagnosed HIV cases, the healthcare system must integrate routine HIV screening across all care settings. Screening should not be confined to primary care or limited to traditionally high-risk populations, such as individuals residing in areas of high prevalence or receiving prenatal care. Instead, HIV testing should be normalized and extended to specialty practices, including dermatology, otolaryngology, and general surgery, particularly for patients who may not routinely engage with primary care.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Relevant State Laws in the United States
retrieved_docs.content: In a nonhealthcare setting, such as community outreach programs, informed consent with the option to decline testing and information regarding sites that provide anonymous testing in the community must be obtained. In the event of a positive test result, the patient must receive appropriate care and medical support services, information on the importance of notifying partners who may have been exposed, and ways to prevent transmission. In the event of a negative test result, every effort should be made to notify the patient and provide information regarding ways to prevent the acquisition of HIV. In a healthcare setting, if the patient is discharged before the results are available, the county health department must notify the test subject to fulfill the responsibility after a positive test result.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: See " Monitoring, Surveillance, and Reporting " under  "Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes" in this article for further information on the 95-95-95 goals. See StatPearls' companion topics, " HIV and AIDS " and " HIV Antiretroviral Therapy ," for further information on these topics.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Staging systems can be used for clinical or surveillance purposes to assess the rate of progression to more advanced stages, assist in monitoring the HIV burden at a population level, plan for prevention and care, and evaluate interventions. The CDC published the current definitions of HIV surveillance cases in 2014. The definitions of surveillance cases are not intended for clinical decision-making. HIV testing confirms the diagnosis and determines acute infection in stage 0, and the CD4+ count determines stages 1 to 3. All surveillance cases are assumed to be HIV-1 unless laboratory evidence indicates HIV-2. The criteria for stage 0 supersede and are independent of the criteria used for other stages. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Relevant State Laws in the United States
retrieved_docs.content: Unconfirmed test results must not be presented to the patient as an HIV diagnosis. The rationale for releasing unconfirmed results must be documented in the chart. Confirmatory testing must be obtained, and the results must be communicated to the individual tested. Positive HIV test results, after confirmatory testing, may only be released to the tested individual or their designated legally authorized representative. Results must be reported to the State Health Department following rules for reporting and controlling disease. Results may also be shared with clinicians who use semen or body parts from an infected individual, health facility committees for purposes of program monitoring and evaluation, and authorized researchers and epidemiologists, as proscribed under appropriate statutes and procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: PrEP for HIV is an essential part of clinical HIV prevention and health maintenance. The rationale for PrEP, epidemiology of HIV transmission in high-risk populations, the identification of those at risk, treatment options, monitoring of PrEP, management of complications, client support for adherence to therapy, and individual, programmatic, and systems methods to enhance healthcare team outcomes in providing PrEP to clients are integral to HIV prevention. Using quality improvement and public health lenses ensures maximal benefit to those most at risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1878
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a major route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdine‑mesylate and efavirenz have the potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. In contrast, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this immunosuppressive medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in both preventing HIV transmission and selecting safe therapeutic options for patients living with the virus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1211
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated, reflecting the need to avoid this immunosuppressive agent in this population. Conversely, the antiretroviral drugs delavirdin and delavirdin‑mesylate are noted for their potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. Understanding these relationships—risk behaviors, drug contraindications, and preventive therapies—is essential for effective patient management and infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2665
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, and etravirine have been identified as medications that may prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. Understanding both the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use and the preventive capabilities of these drugs is essential for effective patient counseling, risk reduction, and public‑health interventions aimed at curbing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2086
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. The antiretroviral agents delavirdin‑mesilát and elvitegravir are both recognized for their potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader strategies to reduce the risk associated with behaviors such as shared needle use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2805
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة); in patients with AIDS, pulmonary opportunistic infections and other HIV‑related lung complications frequently produce atypical or abnormal radiographic appearances, making chest imaging an important diagnostic and monitoring tool in the management of this immunocompromised condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission. In the context of HIV prevention and therapy, delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir are identified as agents that may help prevent acquisition of the virus, while fostemsavir is recognized for its potential to treat established HIV infection. Conversely, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships supports informed decision‑making in both preventive strategies and therapeutic management of HIV‑related care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض, and a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي is also clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض. Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical link between this symptom complex and HIV infection, indicating that patients presenting with low white‑blood‑cell counts should be evaluated for underlying HIV disease and related symptomatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. The use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Several antiretroviral agents are associated with the prevention or treatment of HIV infection. Delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir have been reported to have preventive activity against HIV acquisition, and raltegravir is also recognized for its ability to prevent HIV infection. Cabotegravir, in contrast, is used primarily for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir all belong to the class of integrase inhibitors, a group of systematic anti‑infective agents that target the viral integrase enzyme to block viral replication. Understanding these relationships assists clinicians in selecting appropriate preventive strategies, therapeutic regimens, and avoiding contraindicated drugs for individuals living with or at risk for HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1229
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesilát and etravirine have demonstrated the ability to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. Understanding the connection between shared needle use and HIV transmission, together with the preventive capabilities of delavirdin‑mesilát and etravirine, is essential for effective public‑health interventions and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, highlighting a major route of transmission that requires targeted harm‑reduction strategies. Pharmacologic agents such as delavirdin‑mesilát, lamivudin, and zidovudin have each been identified as having preventive potential against HIV infection, underscoring the role of antiretroviral medications in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and infection control. Integrating needle‑sharing risk mitigation with the use of these preventive drugs is essential for reducing new HIV cases and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1414
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is a viral disease that is contraindicated with the use of methotrexate sodium, indicating that patients with HIV should not be treated with this immunosuppressive medication. The condition is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission that underscores the importance of harm‑reduction strategies in prevention. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesylate and elvitegravir have been identified as having the potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and early intervention efforts. Understanding these relationships—avoidance of methotrexate sodium in HIV‑positive individuals, the risk posed by needle sharing, and the preventive capabilities of delavirdin‑mesylate and elvitegravir—is essential for optimizing patient care and reducing new infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1662
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and elvitegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as integrase inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, these agents target the HIV integrase enzyme and are employed in systemic antiretroviral therapy to prevent viral DNA integration into host cells, thereby contributing to the management of HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.
